title: Co-Development Materials
text:
The following summarizes courses I have developed or taught for universities, professional development courses, conference mini-courses, and self-guided learning. Like my analytical projects, I take a highly collaborative approach to instruction, and most classes are co-developed with complementary expertise. At present, only brief course descriptions are provided, but syllabi and course materials will be uploaded in the future.




  

The Design Justice Network Principles are a framework for rethinking how design processes impact communities, redistribute power, and imagine futures rooted in equity and liberation. These principles center the voices, knowledge systems, and lived experiences of communities who are most impacted by design decisions.
Grounded in intersectional analysis, Design Justice asks us to reconsider who is involved in design, who benefits from it, and who may be harmed or excluded. It offers a pathway to create processes and tools that are community-led, culturally grounded, and oriented toward collective flourishing.
Find the Principles Here
The principles emphasize:

Centering the experiences and leadership of those most impacted by design outcomes
Building community capacity, not just extracting knowledge
Designing with, not for, and valuing different forms of expertise
Challenging the matrix of domination, including racism, colonialism, heteropatriarchy, ableism, and economic exploitation
Ensuring meaningful participation across all stages of design
Promoting sustainable, community-controlled outcomes
Supporting collective liberation through design that honors many worlds, many histories, and many futures

These principles are used around the world in design, engineering, education, public health, environmental science, and community technology. Integrating this framework into our work helps shift power, create accountable collaboration, and ensure that design processes contribute to justice rather than reinforcing inequity.




Co-development requires relationships grounded in reciprocity rather than one-sided knowledge extraction, or “parachute” science. In ecological modeling, this means building collaborations where partners benefit in tangible ways, whether through shared decision-making, capacity building, community-relevant data products, or long-term stewardship of outcomes.
This approach re-frames ecological modeling as a shared learning process rather than a technical service. In practice, I emphasize:

Early and ongoing engagement to identify community priorities
Shared authority in defining modeling goals, outputs, and acceptable uses
Equitable distribution of benefits, including training, data access, and authorship where appropriate
Technology Transfer
Continual reflection on whether the collaboration remains reciprocal

This relationship ethic aligns with principles of community science, Tribal sovereignty, and responsible environmental decision-making.




Trust is foundational to collaborative ecological modeling. Technical work alone cannot sustain a partnership; communication and relationship building must be ongoing and intentional. Relationships are best built from other connections or “bridges” to connect you to others to initiate a relationship.
My instruction highlights:

Practices for building trust across disciplines and cultures
Approaches for communicating uncertainty, risk, and trade-offs
Collaborative decision-making tools, such as conceptual modeling, shared mapping, and iterative feedback loops
Facilitation strategies that create space for many voices and many forms of expertise
Approaches to conflict navigation, especially in community-embedded environmental work

Effective modeling depends on shared understanding, shared language, and shared ownership of what the model is intended to accomplish.




The ABT narrative framework provides a simple and powerful structure for scientific communication. It distills a story into three essential beats: And, But, and Therefore. This format helps translate complex ecological or modeling concepts into clear and actionable insights that people can easily follow.
ABT supports collaborative work by creating a shared narrative foundation. Rather than listing data in long chains, ABT prompts us to articulate context, tension, and a meaningful path forward. These three elements match the way people naturally understand environmental problems.


I use ABT to re-frame and re-scope ecological modeling projects through the perspectives of local community members, including Indigenous community members whose stewardship, lived experience, and cultural knowledge shape the ecosystem. Many modeling projects begin with technical objectives that do not fully reflect community priorities. ABT helps re-position the work so that community-defined concerns lead the narrative.
Here is an example from my previous work on the Penobscot River Estuary:
And:
People who live along the Penobscot River rely on the estuary for cultural practices, food traditions, and community identity, and they continue to protect and care for the river through long-standing stewardship. Community members, including the Wabanaki Nations and the Penobscot Nation, have deep knowledge of fish behavior, seasonal patterns, and habitat conditions.
But:
Fish in the estuary continue to carry mercury at levels that limit access, safety, and cultural use. Community members see that current restoration efforts often focus on where the most contaminated sediment sits on the riverbed, but these efforts do not always address where fish actually encounter contamination as they migrate, feed, and rest. This mismatch creates a gap between technical remediation strategies and community needs for safe and accessible fish.
Therefore:
Re-framing the problem around fish exposure, community access, and cultural well-being supports solutions that focus on the places and processes where fish interact with contamination. This shift helps guide decision making toward strategies that reduce exposure, improve access to traditional foods, and support long-term community and Tribal priorities for the river.
This form of storytelling helps align ecological models with the experiences and values of the people who live within the ecosystem, rather than with technical concerns alone.



I recommend modelers use ABT to:

Articulate model purpose and clarify decision contexts
Communicate trade-offs and limitations in ways that resonate with local communities
Build concise narratives for presentations, reports, and community meetings
Connect technical outputs to policy, cultural priorities, and community-defined goals
Re-scope project objectives so they reflect community perspectives, not only technical or regulatory ones




Read More About the ABT Framework Here
Download Worksheets Here
ABT strengthens communication and co-development because it creates stories that are coherent, compelling, and grounded in the lived experiences of local communities and Indigenous knowledge holders.






  

Data visualization is a central component of ecological modeling because it helps partners interpret patterns, explore scenarios, and make informed decisions. My instruction focuses on creating transparent and accessible visual products that support communication and decision making. Visualizations also carry inherent and sometimes unintended connotations that influence how people interpret information. Choices in color, scale, layout, and symbol design can shape meaning, emotion, and accessibility, which can either clarify data or introduce misunderstanding. A careful and intentional approach ensures that visualizations remain truthful, respectful, and supportive of community decision processes.
Core topics include:

Designing intuitive visualizations for interdisciplinary audiences
Using color, scale, and layout to highlight ecological patterns
Animating spatial and temporal processes, such as migration, flow dynamics, and contaminant movement
Building reproducible workflows in R and Python
Developing visual tools for workshops, dashboards, and interactive model exploration



Best Practices
Charts & Graphs
What is the significance of color in data visualization?
Visualization is treated not just as aesthetics, but as a co-development tool that shapes understanding and guides collective insight.






  

Collaborative modeling must respect data sovereignty, especially when working with Tribal Nations, local communities, or groups with culturally sensitive knowledge. This section introduces ethical and legal considerations surrounding data ownership, access, governance, and use.
Instruction covers:

Principles of Indigenous Data Sovereignty and community-controlled research
OCAP, CARE, and FAIR frameworks and how they interact
Developing data-sharing agreements that reflect community priorities
Ensuring culturally appropriate use of knowledge, stories, and sensitive information
Protecting intellectual property arising from co-produced models and datasets



Indigenous Data Sovereignty & Governance
Data Sovereignty
Indigenous Data Goverance Principles
Respecting data sovereignty is not only an ethical commitment, but essential to building trust, strengthening collaborations, and ensuring that models are used in ways that align with community values.

title: Coding 101 for Ecological Modeling
text:
Core Coding Skills



Coding is the backbone of ecological modeling.
It enables transparent methods, reproducible workflows, automated analyses, and models that reflect real ecological processes. Most importantly, diverse coding skills allow knowledge to move easily across disciplines, increasing model utility by making tools accessible to different communities and management audiences. Different fields use their own programming conventions and software ecosystems, so knowing multiple languages improves communication, interdisciplinary collaboration, and technology transfer. Coding is also essential for designing interactive, accessible communication and decision-support applications.
Whether simulating fish behavior, building hydrodynamic models, analyzing sediment transport, or creating decision-support tools, coding is what makes ecological research scalable, clear, and open.
Below are the core tools I use across hydrodynamics, habitat modeling, sediment transport, groundwater modeling, and agent-based models — organized into a clean, easy-to-navigate toolkit.





R Programming


R is built for statistical analysis, data visualization, reproducible documents, and geospatial workflows. It excels at modeling, spatial analysis, and generating publication-ready figures. It also supports knitting Word documents, PDFs, and interactive HTML outputs, making it a flexible tool for scientific communication.


How I use it: Habitat suitability modeling (HSI rasters), tidal and discharge analysis, Integrated Fitness (IF) mapping, SPM trend analysis, spatial autocorrelation (Moran’s I and LISA), and producing RMarkdown/Quarto reports for transparent, reproducible science.

Explore R Tips →




Python


Python supports large-scale scientific computing, automation, hydrodynamic modeling, and environmental data processing. It is strongly aligned with reproducible open science, especially through notebooks and living documents. Python also generates fast, high-resolution outputs for groundwater simulations and particle-tracking analyses.


How I use it: Groundwater–surface water modeling (MODFLOW/MODPATH using FloPy), automating analysis pipelines, processing rasters, forecasting climate and fisheries trends, and developing the GoFish modeling toolkit.

Explore Python Tips →




NetLogo


NetLogo is a specialty platform for agent-based modeling, enabling spatially explicit behavioral simulations and rule-based ecological interactions. It supports model co-development and is ideal for answering complex ecological questions that combine hydrodynamics, behavior, and environmental gradients.


How I use it: Core engine for river herring, striped bass, and sturgeon behavioral models, including selective tidal stream transport (STST), staging, predator–prey dynamics, spawning behavior, contaminant exposure pathways, and social learning simulations used in workshops and manuscripts.

Explore NetLogo Tips → Explore Open-Source Library →




MATLAB


MATLAB excels at numerical analysis, matrix operations, and advanced signal processing, making it essential for hydrodynamics and environmental physics. It is widely used in coastal engineering for processing field data and preparing inputs for coupled hydrodynamic models.


How I use it: Wavelet decomposition to isolate tidal frequencies, SPM flux analysis, sediment transport metrics, ADCP calibration, and discharge–tidal harmonics assessment — all core components of my Penobscot hydrodynamics research.

Explore MATLAB Tips →




Version Control


Git and GitHub enable transparent, collaborative, and reproducible science through version tracking, branching, merging, and workflow documentation. They support open, community-centered model development and improve scientific transparency.


How I use it: Managing all major modeling repositories including GoFish, hydrodynamic scripts, ABM modules, Quarto websites, workshop materials, and forecasting models. Version control also supports co-development with Tribal partners and federal collaborators.

Explore GitHub Utility →




HTML / CSS / JS


HTML structures content, CSS controls styling, and JavaScript adds interactivity — together they enable interactive, accessible scientific communication tools. These languages are essential for modern model communication, user interfaces, and knowledge-transfer applications.


How I use it: Building Quarto websites, interactive modeling pages, animated hero banners, pixel-art interfaces, workshop instruction materials, and public-facing documentation for GoFish and Penobscot modeling projects.

Explore HTML Resources →

title: Katherine Daza
text:
Ecology and Environmental Sciences, University of Maine
National Science Foundation: 3-D Ecosystem Science National Research Traineeship Fellow




About My Work

I work at the intersection of hydrodynamics, sediment and contaminant transport, and fish behavior. My research focuses on how exposure actually emerges in real estuarine systems, not just where contamination exists on a map.
I approach modeling as a way to make processes visible. Instead of treating risk as static, I build frameworks that show how movement, interaction, habitat, and environmental variability combine to shape outcomes over time. My goal is to create tools that are transparent, reproducible, and genuinely useful for management and restoration.






My Modeling Philosophy



Transparent modeling. I build models that clearly state assumptions, uncertainty, and limitations so they can be critiqued, interpreted, and improved rather than treated as black boxes.
Co-development. My work is shaped through collaboration with Tribal partners, federal agencies, local experts, and resource managers so that model structure and outputs reflect both ecological realism and decision context.
Simplicity in complexity. Complex systems do not require overly complicated tools. I focus on isolating the mechanisms that matter most while keeping models accessible, adaptable, and communicable.
Decision support. I design models to help people think through risk, compare scenarios, and identify where management actions may be most effective.

title: Katherine Daza
text:
Research Areas





Hydrodynamics
Flow regimes, tidal asymmetry, river discharge, and sediment transport as drivers of material movement through estuarine systems.






Agent-Based Modeling
Behavior, migration, schooling, and trophic interactions as mechanisms that transform potential exposure into realized risk.






Habitat Modeling
Life-stage-specific habitat suitability frameworks linking environmental gradients with when and where fish can persist.






Co-Development
Collaborative model building with Tribal partners, agencies, and communities to improve transparency, relevance, and use.

title: Katherine Daza
text:
Featured Tools


Alongside publications and reports, I build public-facing tools and modeling products that support interpretation, communication, and applied decision-making.




Mercury Risk Explorer
Interactive application visualizing how hydrodynamics, habitat, and fish behavior structure mercury exposure risk in estuaries.



GoFish Toolkit
Behavioral modeling toolkit for migratory fish with functions for movement, energetics, exposure, and ecological interaction.







HyporheicFloPy
Automated groundwater and hyporheic modeling workflow built to support restoration and aquatic systems analysis.



River Herring Habitat Model
Transparent habitat suitability framework for alewife and blueback herring across life stages and changing estuarine conditions.

title: Curriculum Vitae
text:
Vanessa M. Quintana
Applied Ecological Modeler · Coastal Systems Scientist · Geospatial Analyst

📧 Email GitHub LinkedIn ORCID

Applied ecological modeler developing decision-support tools for federal agencies, Tribal partners, and coastal resource managers, with over five years of experience in hydrodynamic and ecological modeling. My work integrates hydrodynamic modeling, spatial analysis, and agent-based modeling to translate complex environmental systems into actionable insight for environmental management, restoration, and policy.

Led design of Tribal decision-support agent-based models integrating hydrodynamics and fish behavior

University of Maine

title: Ecological Modeling Basics funciono?
text: Ecological models help us understand how living systems behave and respond to change. They connect observations, data, and ecological theory to represent the processes that shape populations, habitats, and ecosystems. Rather than acting as perfect replicas of nature, they provide structured ways to explore complexity, test ideas, and support management decisions.

title: Ecological Modeling Basics funciono?
text:
Ecological Modeling

Overview
Ecological modeling includes a wide range of approaches, from simple conceptual diagrams to fully coupled physical and biological simulations. Different approaches are useful for different questions, scales, and management needs.
My work focuses on developing ecological models that integrate biological behavior with environmental and physical processes. I build transparent and reproducible modeling frameworks that support science based decision making, assessment of ecological risk, and community engaged management.

Major model types
Habitat Models
Spatial models that quantify environmental suitability and identify critical habitat across life stages and environmental conditions.
Agent Based Models
Individual based simulations that capture behavior, movement, and interactions to explore emergent ecological patterns and system level responses.
Coupled Models
Integrated physical biological models that connect hydrodynamics, habitat conditions, and contaminant exposure to evaluate how environmental change shapes population and ecosystem outcomes.




Habitat Modeling


What Are Habitat Models?
Habitat models describe how environmental conditions support, limit, or structure where organisms can survive, grow, and reproduce. These models translate environmental variables such as depth, temperature, velocity, salinity, and substrate into estimates of habitat availability or quality. Habitat models are widely used in conservation planning, restoration design, species assessments, and understanding how ecosystems respond to environmental change.
Habitat models range from simple conceptual frameworks to fully spatial, GIS based analyses. They help identify critical areas for life stages, evaluate habitat changes over space and time, and provide a foundation for linking physical processes with biological needs.


Habitat Suitability Indices (HSIs)
Habitat Suitability Indices (HSIs) are a common tool for evaluating habitat quality.
An HSI represents how suitable a particular location is for a species or life stage on a scale from zero to one. Values close to one indicate highly suitable conditions, while values near zero indicate unsuitable habitat.
HSIs combine multiple environmental variables, each translated through species specific suitability curves. These curves describe how closely a given condition matches the known preferences or tolerances of the species. By combining these curves, HSIs allow researchers to map habitat quality across entire landscapes or river systems.


HSIs are useful because they allow researchers to:

evaluate habitat availability for different life stages
identify areas that may serve as critical habitat
compare conditions across years or scenarios
explore how environmental change affects habitat suitability
support restoration, management, and ecological forecasting




Purpose of Habitat Modeling
Habitat models help translate environmental data into biologically meaningful information. They are especially valuable for species with complex life cycles, migratory patterns, or sensitivity to environmental change. These models support decision making by identifying where conditions meet species requirements and how these conditions shift under different scenarios.
For anadromous species, habitat models help reveal how hydrodynamics, temperature, salinity, and flow shape migration pathways and spawning conditions. When combined with behavioral models or contaminant assessments, habitat models serve as the foundation for understanding ecological risk and system dynamics.




Agent Based Modeling


What is an Agent Based Model?
Agent based models (ABMs) simulate the actions and interactions of individual organisms or “agents” moving through their environment. Each agent follows a set of simple rules related to movement, behavior, or decision making. When many agents operate at once, these individual rules can produce complex system level patterns that are difficult to predict from observation alone.
ABMs are useful for exploring behavioral ecology, migration pathways, risk exposure, predator and prey dynamics, and how organism level decisions scale to population or ecosystem outcomes. These models make it possible to represent variation, learning, spatial structure, and individual responses to environmental change in ways that support ecological understanding and management.



Why Use ABMs?






ABMs represent individual behavior and decision making in ways that other modeling approaches cannot. They require advanced integration of ecology, behavior, spatial processes, and computation, along with careful validation to ensure that emergent patterns reflect real system dynamics. This makes ABMs demanding to build but exceptionally powerful for understanding complex ecological questions such as migration, risk exposure, and habitat selection in dynamic environments.
Agent based models are powerful tools because they allow researchers to:

represent individual variability

link movement and behavior to environmental conditions

explore emergent patterns

test “what if” scenarios that are not possible to replicate in the field

evaluate ecological risk and species responses in dynamic systems

ABMs are often paired with habitat models or physical models to understand how biology and hydrodynamics interact across space and time.



Emergent Behaviors
One of the defining strengths of agent based modeling is the ability to observe emergent behavior, which occurs when simple individual rules create complex patterns at the group or system level. We can then examine these emergent properties to understand system-level responses.
Below are two examples demonstrating emergent behavior using different numbers of agents.






Emergent behavior with many agents interacting simultaneously







Emergent behavior with two agents following basic interaction rules




Why ABMs Are Difficult to Build

They require translating ecological processes into explicit individual rules

They must integrate movement, behavior, and environment at fine scales

They demand careful attention to stochasticity, uncertainty, and variability

They generate large spatial and temporal datasets that require efficient computation

They need rigorous testing to ensure that emergent patterns match real world observations

They often must combine multiple domains such as environmental dynamics, animal behavior, physiological limits, and ecological interactions.




What Makes ABMs Unique
Agent based models offer capabilities that are not achievable with most other ecological modeling approaches. They require a blend of ecological understanding, behavioral theory, spatial analysis, and computational skill that makes them both powerful and technically demanding.
ABMs stand out because they allow researchers to represent how individuals perceive their environment, make decisions, and interact with physical and biological processes. This perspective creates opportunities to study behaviors, risks, and system responses that are invisible in population level or equation based models.
ABMs also require advanced model design, careful rule construction, and rigorous validation to ensure that individual decisions scale realistically to emergent outcomes. This level of detail and transparency demands a strong understanding of both ecological processes and computational implementation.


Why This Expertise Matters
Because ABMs simulate decision making and behavior, they can capture risk pathways, migration patterns, predator interactions, and exposure processes that other models cannot represent. This capability is especially important in systems where individual behavior determines ecological outcomes, such as tidal river migration, contaminant exposure, and habitat selection.
Developing these models requires skills in simulation design, ecological theory, spatial modeling, and transparent validation. My work combines these elements to create agent based models that are both scientifically grounded and directly useful for management planning.




What Are Coupled Models?
Coupled models combine multiple processes into a single integrated framework.
These models link physical, chemical, and biological systems to explore how different components of an environment interact across space and time. In ecological applications, coupled models often blend hydrodynamics, habitat conditions, contaminant transport, and species behavior.
By pairing these elements, researchers can ask questions about how environmental change influences ecological outcomes, how individual decisions scale to population effects, and how physical processes shape risk and exposure.



Why Use Coupled Models?
Coupled models are valuable because they allow researchers to:

represent interactions between physical and biological systems

simulate environments that shift over space, time, and tidal cycles

assess ecological risk under multiple scenarios

capture processes that cannot be studied through a single model type

evaluate management actions, restoration strategies, or environmental change

They provide a framework for understanding complex systems where no single model can capture the full story.



Integration and Complexity
Coupled models require careful coordination of multiple data streams, modeling domains, and temporal scales. Integrating hydrodynamics, habitat suitability, and behavior requires:

aligning spatial grids and coordinate systems

synchronizing time steps across model types

ensuring feedback between processes is realistic and biologically meaningful

validating each component independently and in combination

managing large computational workloads and high-resolution datasets

This level of integration makes coupled models powerful, but also technically complex. Successful implementation requires strong interdisciplinary knowledge, transparent workflows, and reproducible coding practices.



Cost–Benefit of Coupled Modeling
While complex, coupled models offer significant advantages:

Benefits

more realistic representation of ecological systems

improved understanding of risk, exposure, and environmental change

insight into processes that cannot be observed directly

support for management decisions based on integrated system behavior



Costs

higher computational demands

greater model development time

increased need for interdisciplinary expertise

more detailed validation and scenario testing

larger, more complex datasets to maintain

Despite these challenges, the benefits often outweigh the costs, especially in systems where physical processes strongly influence ecological outcomes. Coupled approaches allow researchers to understand not only where and when organisms move, but why those movements occur in a dynamic environment.




Coupled Models Address Complex Environmental Problems



Estuaries like the Penobscot River are shaped by spatial patterns of contamination, tidal asymmetry, sediment resuspension, and shifting habitat conditions.
These processes operate across scales and cannot be captured by any single model.




Conceptual diagram of spatial and temporal drivers in a macrotidal estuary



Spatial and temporal processes that drive contamination transport, habitat dynamics, and anadromous fish behavior in macrotidal estuaries.


Coupled models are valuable because they allow researchers to:

represent interactions between physical and biological processes

simulate environments that shift over space, time, and tidal cycles

assess ecological risk under multiple scenarios

evaluate where, when, and why exposure or habitat use occurs

guide restoration and management decisions with integrated system insight

title: Modeling Projects
text:
A coupled hydrodynamic and agent-based modeling framework designed to evaluate how environmental dynamics and fish behavior structure contaminant exposure risk for anadromous fish in the Penobscot River estuary. The model integrates Delft3D FM hydrodynamic outputs with NetLogo-based behavioral agents to produce time-varying, spatially explicit exposure estimates.

Agent-Based Modeling Hydrodynamics Contaminant Transport NetLogo




Life-stage-specific habitat suitability modeling for alewife and blueback herring across changing estuarine conditions. The framework links environmental gradients — temperature, salinity, depth, flow — with species-specific life-stage requirements to produce dynamic habitat maps across the tidal estuary.

Habitat Suitability R GIS GitHub Dashboard




Automation of groundwater modeling workflows using FloPy and Python to evaluate hyporheic zone ecological benefits for stream restoration projects. The HyporheicFloPy tool reduced model runtimes from days to minutes while producing reproducible, documented workflows for federal project use.

Python FloPy Groundwater USACE Documentation

title: Modeling Projects
text:
Tools and Applications


Mercury Risk Explorer


An interactive Shiny application that visualizes how hydrodynamics, habitat, and fish behavior structure mercury exposure risk in estuaries. Designed for both scientific communication and stakeholder engagement, the app lets users explore exposure probability across tidal phases, seasons, and behavioral scenarios.

R Shiny Decision Support Interactive Launch App



GoFish Modeling Toolkit


A behavioral modeling toolkit for migratory fish with functions covering movement, energetics, exposure, trophic interactions, and ecological risk. Built to be modular and extensible for researchers working on anadromous species across different estuarine contexts.

R Package Behavioral Modeling Open Source Documentation



Community Facing Workshops and Tools


Alongside research products, I develop educational materials, workshop curricula, and public-facing dashboards for Tribal partners, resource managers, and researchers. This includes GitHub workflow tutorials for federal scientists and participatory modeling workshops designed for mixed technical and non-technical audiences.

Outreach Co-Development Training GitHub Tutorials

title: Research
text:
My dissertation investigates how tidal behavior and contaminated suspended particulate matter influence anadromous fish habitat and mercury exposure in the Penobscot River system.

Dissertation Focus


Using the Penobscot River as a case study, this work develops coupled hydrodynamic and behavioral modeling frameworks to understand how exposure risk emerges from the interaction between material transport, habitat availability, and organism movement — rather than treating contamination as fixed in space.


Key themes:



Hydrodynamic drivers of sediment and contaminant transport


Seasonal habitat suitability for river herring life stages


Behavioral pathways of realized contaminant exposure


Community-engaged and co-developed ecological modeling

title: Research
text:
Current Research Areas


Hydrodynamics and Contaminant Transport


This work examines how river discharge, flood tides, and tidal asymmetry structure material transport and exposure opportunity in estuarine environments. Hydrodynamic outputs from Delft3D FM inform spatial and temporal distributions of contaminated suspended particulate matter across the estuary.




Habitat Suitability and Exposure Risk


I use life-stage-specific habitat suitability models to evaluate when and where high-quality habitat overlaps with contaminated sediment. This produces spatially explicit exposure opportunity maps that vary with season, discharge, and tidal phase.




Coupled Behavioral and Hydrodynamic Modeling


I developed a coupled hydrodynamic and agent-based framework to test how migration, foraging, schooling, and predator–prey interactions influence realized exposure risk. The framework translates environmental conditions into organism-level outcomes over time.




Co-Development and Tribal Engagement


Model structure and behavioral rules are developed collaboratively with Tribal partners, incorporating Traditional Ecological Knowledge alongside observational data. This shapes both the scientific questions asked and the tools built to answer them.
